title: Bob Langer on Overcoming Rejection and Changing ...
text:
so I was asked for the first time in my
life to give a talk at a major
scientific meeting uh in early
1976 I practiced this talk it was 20
minute talk for for for two weeks into a
tape recorder and I got up and I was 27
years old I remember and I was giving
this talk to this really distinguished
audience of chemists and chemical
engineers in Michigan and I thought at
the end of the talk I actually did okay
I didn't forget too much what I was
going to say I didn't stammer too much
so I thought when I was done with this
talk all these older scientists being
nice people they would want to encourage
me this young guy but when I got done I
stepped off the podium and a whole bunch
of them came up to me and they said we
don't believe anything you just said if
you could go to the next slide what they
said was just what the Ala people said
the molecules were too big to get out
they also pointed out to me that I was
using organic solvents like which I was
ethylene methylene chloride ethanol and
they said that's going to denature any
of these things like peptides proteins
or nucleic acids and actually sadly for
me things went downhill from there you
know I I uh wrote research grants
because I wanted to support the work and
my first nine were rejected and very
soundly I remember one of them came back
from the NIH and they said well Dr
Langer is an engineer he knows nothing
about biology and even less about
oncology now I thought I knew enough
about math to know that wasn't true but
I mean I not that I had any say in it so
I didn't get any of those grants also I
love being a postdoc I really love being
a children's hospital I was the only
engineer there I learned so much but um
you know a lot of my friends they start
saying Bob probably you shouldn't spend
your whole life doing that and I did
think okay well maybe I'll apply for
faculty positions so since I'm a
chemical engineer I applied for chemical
engineering faculty positions and no
University in the world would hire me
they actually said the opposite or not
the opposite of an they said well an
engineer doesn't do bio experimental
biow work I mean you know they should be
doing energy or math but but the stuff
that I was proposing made no sense to be
done in a chemical engineering
department so I ended up getting a job
in the Nutrition department I think I
got it because the head of that
department Nevan scrimshire very famous
MIT Professor he was kind of like what
I'd call a benevolent dictator kind of
Professor he um they sometimes they have
that at MIT nean you know he was a great
man and but he uh he liked me so he
offered me a job as an assist assistant
professor but um he didn't ask anybody
else in the department what they thought
which might have been okay except for
one thing which was the year after I
joined the Department he left so a lot
of the senior faculty decided to give me
advice their advice is I should leave
too in fact on the next
slide this is uh a couple years ago I
got some award and they asked Mike
marletta he was a professor in Nutrition
department along with me at the time uh
he ended up having a fantastic career he
uh was a head of scripts he was chair of
Berkeley's chemistry Department member
of the National Academy of Science who
was a very close friend still is and and
and he made some comments about what it
was like to be with me in uh in the late
1970s here's what he said he said one
evening I went to a faculty dinner at a
Chinese restaurant with me and some
senior MIT professors he said a senior
scientist had quizzing us while smoking
a cigar he said when the older scientist
Serv my concepts for drug delivery he
blew a cloud of smoke in my face and
said you better start looking for
another job I I remember that well but
anyhow that wasn't very good so I had a
very inauspicious beginning um I'm still
here though so it's it it it worked out
okay you know you sort of put your head
down and you keep trying uh regardless
of what other people said
[Music]
